## Success

This section defines what successful completion of the project looks like, how it will be measured, and how it can evolve beyond the initial funding period.

---

## Definition of Done

The project will be considered successful when the following outputs are fully completed and delivered:

- A fully developed beginner-level R curriculum in both English and Hausa  
- A complete offline-first learning toolkit that can be used without internet access  
- A facilitator training guide enabling independent replication of the program  
- A publicly accessible open-source repository containing all materials and documentation  
- Successful completion of a pilot program in Kano involving learners and facilitators  
- A final evaluation report documenting outcomes, feedback, and lessons learned  

Success is defined not only by the creation of materials but also by the successful training of learners and facilitators in a real-world environment.

---

## Measuring Success

Success will be measured using both quantitative and qualitative indicators:

### Quantitative Metrics
- Number of learners trained (target: 50–100)  
- Number of facilitators trained (target: 8–12)  
- Completion rate of participants across training modules  
- Number of successfully completed exercises and projects  

### Qualitative Metrics
- Participant feedback on clarity, accessibility, and usability of materials  
- Facilitator feedback on ease of teaching and adoption  
- Observed improvement in learner confidence and understanding of R  
- Engagement levels during pilot sessions  

Additional success indicators include the usability of the offline toolkit in environments with limited or no internet access.

---

## Future Work

This project is designed as a scalable and expandable model that can be extended beyond the initial pilot.

Future development may include:

- Expansion of curriculum to intermediate and advanced R topics  
- Adaptation of materials into additional local languages  
- Scaling the program to other regions in Northern Nigeria and beyond  
- Integration of the toolkit into schools, universities, and community learning centers  
- Development of specialized R tracks (e.g., data visualization, data analysis, research applications)  
- Growth of a wider facilitator network to support long-term sustainability  

By releasing all materials as open-source resources, the project ensures that communities globally can adapt, improve, and extend the work beyond its initial scope.
